1: The chapter addresses the šōʿᵉrîm — gatekeepers. The institutional organization of gatekeeping is central to the Chronicler's portrait of David's preparations. lᵉmaḥlᵉqôt haššōʿᵉrîm — “for the divisions of the gatekeepers.” The root šāʿar (gate) forms the denominative participle: those assigned to the gates. Meshelemiah (= Shelemiah) son of Kore belongs to the sons of Asaph (though textually Asaph here may mean a Korahite clan; the LXX differs). The Korahites (mibᵉnê-Qōraḥ) trace to Korah, whose rebellion in Num 16 cost him his life but whose lineage continued (Num 26:11) and produced Psalms 42-49.
2: Meshelemiah's sons: Zechariah the firstborn (bᵉkôr), followed by six others. The listing by birth order signals legitimacy claims and inheritance rights for gatekeeper positions. bᵉkôr (firstborn) carries both status and responsibility in Israelite social organization — the firstborn gatekeeper presumably holds authority over the post.
3: The seven sons of Meshelemiah named (vv2-3). The number seven for the firstborn gatekeeper's sons parallels the fullness of provision: sufficient personnel to staff the gate rotation. The names are formulaic: Elam, Jehohanan, Elioenai, Jaaziah, Elihoenai, Zechariah, Hashbaddanah appear in variant forms in Ezra-Nehemiah contexts, indicating the gatekeeper families persisted into the post-exilic period.
4: Obed-edom's sons: the Obed-edom who served as host to the ark (1 Chr 13:14, 15:18-24) is now listed as the father of eight sons who become gatekeepers. ûḇānîm ṭôḇāh ʾĕlōhîm lô — the narrative aside, “God blessed him,” from 13:14, explains the numerous progeny. The eight sons named in vv4-7 (Shemaiah, Jehozabad, Joah, Sachar, Nethanel, Ammiel, Issachar, Peullethai) follow by name, establishing the legitimacy of the Obed-edom gatekeeper clan.
5: The second son Jehozabad is followed by Joah. kî bēraḵô ʾĕlōhîm — the divine blessing on Obed-edom referenced here echoes the explicit statement in 13:14 that YHWH blessed Obed-edom and all his household during the ark's stay. The gatekeeper appointment thus carries the narrative memory of the ark's transit: the family that hosted the ark now guards the temple's gates.
6: Shemaiah the son of Obed-edom (ûlišᵉmayāh bᵉnô) himself has sons listed: rulers (môšᵉlîm) in their father's house, for they were mighty men (gibbōrê ḥayil). The phrase gibbōrê ḥayil (warriors of valor/wealth) applied to gatekeepers is notable — gatekeeper duty in the ancient world required martial capability; the gates controlled access to the sacred space and required defense. The sons listed are described as having rule over their family clan.
7: The sons of Shemaiah: Othni, Rephael, Obed, Elzabad — and then specifically wᵉ ʾeḥāyw Elihu and Semachiah, who are called bᵉnê ḥayil (sons of valor). The distinction between those simply named and those additionally called bᵉnê ḥayil may indicate the latter held special military-gatekeeping functions. The total gatekeeper force from Obed-edom's line is given as 62 in v8.
8: Summary: kol-ʾēlleh mibᵉnê ʾôḇēd ʾĕdôm hēmmāh ûḇᵉnêhem wᵉʾaḥêhem ʾîš-ḥayil baḵōaḥ lāʿăḇōdāh šᵉnayim wᵉšišîm lᵉʾôḇēd ʾĕdôm — 62 men from Obed-edom's line, all men of ability (ʾîš-ḥayil) for the service. The 62 represents one of the larger clan-service numbers in this section. ḥayil in the context of temple service carries both the military connotation (for gate defense) and general capability.
9: Meshelemiah's sons and brothers — 18 strong men (šᵉmōnāh ʿāśār) capable men (bᵉnê ḥayil). The relative sizes of the three gatekeeper families — Meshelemiah 18, Obed-edom 62, Hosah 13 (v11) — reflect actual family sizes rather than an idealized system. The Chronicler preserves authentic personnel records, now organized liturgically.
10: Hosah of the Merarites has sons: his chief (hārōʾš) is Shimri. The note is unusual: kî lōʾ hāyāh bᵉkôr wᵉyaśîmēhû ʾāḇîw lᵉrōʾš — Shimri was not the firstborn, yet his father appointed him as chief. The firstborn convention for succession is here overridden by paternal appointment — a legitimating explanation for why the non-firstborn holds the chief position. This administrative note shows the Chronicler's concern for proper institutional authority.
11: Hosah's other sons: Hilkiah, Tebaliah, Zechariah — total 13 sons and brothers of Hosah (kol-bᵉnê-ḥôsāh ûʾaḥêhem šᵉlōšāh ʿāśār). The combined gatekeeper force from the three families totals 18 + 62 + 13 = 93 men, with additional clan members implied. This was a substantial institutional presence in the temple complex.
12: The gate assignments are made by lots (gôrālôt): lēʾēlleh maḥlᵉqôt haššōʿᵉrîm — these are the divisions of the gatekeepers. lāraʾš laggᵉḇer kaʾăḥîw — for the leaders, the man like his brothers (implying equality of status regardless of family size). The lot system prevents nepotism and attributes the gate assignment to divine providence rather than human preference. Each gate assignment is thereby understood as YHWH's appointment.
13: The lots are cast for each family unit (lᵉḇêt ʾăḇôtām, for their father's house) — both small and great family alike: laqqāṭōn kaggādôl. This democratizing principle — the small family has equal standing in the lot as the large — reflects the theological understanding that temple service is not distributed by human power but by divine ordering.
14: The lot fell (wayyippol haggôrāl) for the east gate to Shelemiah. His son Zechariah gets the north gate — described as a man of śeḵel (understanding/wisdom), who advises wisely (yôʿēṣ bᵉśeḵel). The east gate was the primary processional entrance facing the sunrise; its assignment to the Shelemiah/Meshelemiah family is prestigious. The north gate's assignment to Zechariah, characterized specifically for wisdom, suggests a deliberate match of personnel to station.
15: Obed-edom's lot fell to the south gate (haššāʾûl laNegeḇ). His sons are assigned to the storehouse (lāʾasuppîm). The term ʾăsuppîm (store chambers, collecting points) appears in Neh 12:25 and refers to storage facilities adjacent to the gate. The south gate and storehouse assignment gives the Obed-edom clan responsibility for both entry control and material administration.
16: Shuppim and Hosah have the west gate and the Shallecheth gate (šaʿar haššalleket) — a gate on the ascending causeway (bamsillāh hāʿôlāh). This may be the gate through which waste and refuse were removed from the temple courts. Gatekeepers stand watch (šōmᵉrîm) at corresponding stations: guard beside guard (šōmēr ʾeṣel šōmēr).
17: The daily rotation: eastward, six Levites; northward, four per day; southward, four per day; the storehouse, two and two (šᵉnayim šᵉnayim). The numbers reflect the size and traffic of each gate. The east gate's six-person assignment (double others) befits its status as the primary processional entrance. The paired assignment at the storehouses indicates coordinated oversight of material goods.
18: For the Parbar (western court precinct, lapparbār) — four on the roadway and two at the Parbar itself. The term parbār is a hapax legomenon in 1 Chr 26:18 (and 2 Kgs 23:11), likely an architectural term for a western colonnaded court or suburb. Its exact meaning is uncertain; ancient versions (LXX pharbara, Vulgate pharbar) transliterate rather than translate.
19: Summary: ʾēlleh maḥlᵉqôt haššōʿᵉrîm libᵉnê haQōrāḥî ûlibᵉnê Merārî — these are the divisions of the gatekeepers belonging to the sons of Korah and the sons of Merari. The two levitical lineages — Kohathite (Korahites within Kohath) and Merarite — together staff the entire gate system. The Gershonites, the third major Levite division, are not assigned gate duty here but appear elsewhere in liturgical service.
20: The Levites who oversee the treasuries: wᵉʾăḥêhem halwîm ʿal ʾôṣᵉrôt bêt hāʾĕlōhîm wᵉʿal ʾôṣᵉrôt haqqᵉdāšîm — “and their Levite brothers over the treasuries of the house of God and over the treasuries of the dedicated things.” Two distinct treasuries: (1) ʾôṣᵉrôt bêt hāʾĕlōhîm — the operating treasury of the temple itself; (2) ʾôṣᵉrôt haqqᵉdāšîm — treasury of holy gifts, i.e., dedicated/consecrated donations. The distinction parallels later rabbinic categories of temple funding.
21: The sons of Laadan, who was a Gershonite: the family heads of Laadan the Gershonite are the Jehielites (yᵉḥîʾēlî). Jehiel the son of Laadan (or the Jehielite clan descended from him) leads the Gershonite treasury group. The Gershonites' assignment to treasury oversight rather than gate service reflects the functional differentiation of Levite clans in Chronicles' idealized organization.
22: The sons of Jehiel: Zetham and Joel his brother — ʿal ʾôṣᵉrôt bêt YHWH — over the treasuries of the house of YHWH. The naming of specific individuals responsible for specific treasuries reflects the Chronicler's interest in institutional accountability. Named stewards are identifiable and answerable for their charge.
23: The Amramites, Izharites, Hebronites, and Uzzielites (the four Kohathite families, from Kohath's four sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel, cf. 1 Chr 23:12). These four families together provide the treasury oversight personnel for the inner sanctum functions. The Amramites are especially significant — Moses, Aaron, and Miriam were all Amramites, making this clan the priestly and prophetic lineage.
24: wišᵉḇûʾēl ben-Gēršôm ben-Mōšeh nāgîd ʿal-hāʾôṣārot — Shebuel son of Gershom son of Moses, ruler over the treasuries. The identification of this treasury overseer as the son of Gershom son of Moses is remarkable: a direct grandson of Moses holds institutional authority in David's temple-preparation administration. Nāgîd (ruler, designated leader) is the title used for the king-designate (1 Sam 9:16) and here for the chief treasury officer — a high-status administrative title.
25: The Eliezer line — Rehabiah, Jeshaiah, Joram, Zichri, Shelomoth. Shelomoth (Šᵉlōmōt) and his brothers are assigned over all the dedicated treasuries that David the king, the family heads, the commanders of thousands and hundreds, and the commanders of the army had dedicated. The list traces the genealogy from Eliezer (Moses' second son, Exod 18:4) through five generations to Shelomoth, establishing the legitimacy of this Mosaic lineage for treasury oversight.
26: ʾăšer hiqdîš dāwid haméleḵ ûrāʾšê hāʾāḇôt — what David the king and the family heads had dedicated. The dedicated gifts include booty from wars (mimmilḥāmôt ûmin-haššālāl) — spoils taken from Edom, Moab, Ammon, the Philistines, and Amalek. War spoils dedicated to the temple rather than kept personally demonstrate the sacred purpose of David's campaigns: they were fought not for personal enrichment but for the provision of the temple that David could not himself build.
27: kî mēhaqqᵉrāḇôt ûmin-haššālāl hiqdîšû lᵉḥazzēq lᵉḇêt YHWH — “for from the battles and from the spoil they had dedicated [things] to strengthen (ḥāzaq piel) the house of YHWH.” The verb ḥāzaq (piel: to strengthen, repair, maintain) is the standard term for temple maintenance provision (cf. 2 Kgs 12:5-15; 2 Chr 24:5-13). The dedicated spoils were specifically earmarked for temple upkeep, not general revenue.
28: And all that Samuel the seer (hārōʾeh), Saul son of Kish, Abner son of Ner, and Joab son of Zeruiah had dedicated — everything dedicated was under the care of Shelomoth and his brothers. The inventory of dedicators spans Israel's entire pre-Solomonic history: Samuel (the founding prophet-judge), Saul (the first king), Abner (Saul's general), and Joab (David's general). The convergence of dedicated gifts from across political eras indicates the temple treasury as a national repository transcending individual reigns.
29: The Izharites (haYiṣhārî): Chenaniah and his sons are assigned to external duties (lamlāʾḵāh haḥîṣônāh) — judicial-administrative work beyond the temple. lᵉšōṭᵉrîm ûlᵉšōpᵉṭîm ʿal yiśrāʾēl — as officials (šōṭᵉrîm, enforcement officers) and judges (šōpᵉṭîm) over Israel. The extension of Levitical function into civil judiciary echoes Moses' arrangement in Deut 16:18 and the Jehoshaphat reforms in 2 Chr 19:8-11.
30: The Hebronites: Hashabiah and his brothers — 1,700 capable men (gibbôrê ḥayil) — are assigned over Israel west of the Jordan (maʿăḇar haYardēn maʿᵉrābāh) for all YHWH's work and the king's service. The dual mandate — kol-mᵉlaʾḵet YHWH wᵉʿăḇōdat hamméleḵ — reflects the Chronicler's understanding that civil and sacred administration are intertwined under the Davidic covenant.
31: The Hebronites' chief was Jerijah (Yᵉriyyāhû). In the fortieth year of David's reign (bišnat hāʾarbāʿîm lᵉmalkût dāwid), a search was made among them and mighty men were found at Jazer of Gilead. The datum “fortieth year” anchors this administrative reorganization to the end of David's reign — the final preparation before Solomon's accession. Jazer is a Transjordanian city assigned to the Gadites (Num 32:35).
32: Jerijah had 2,700 capable men (gibbôrê ḥayil) — family heads — whom David the king assigned over the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh for all matters of God and matters of the king. The number 2,700 for Transjordan (vs. 1,700 for Cisjordan in v30) may reflect the more dispersed and exposed nature of the Transjordanian territories requiring larger administrative presence. This final verse closes the gatekeeper-treasury-official section by establishing Levitical oversight over all twelve-tribe territory — a pan-Israelite administrative completeness that anticipates the fully-assembled Israel of Solomon's reign.
